perturbation.original_text: (b) Payment Terms. Except as set forth in such Part 3 of Exhibit "A", payments from Co-Host to NAI shall be due thirty (30) days from the date of invoice. All payments will be made in United States dollars, free of any taxes then currently applicable, at the address designated above by NAI. Late payments shall bear interest at the lesser of: (i) the maximum rate permitted by law, and (ii) the rate of 1.5% per month from the due date until paid.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Payment Terms. Except as set forth in such Part 3 of Exhibit "A", payments from Co-Host to NAI shall be due as determined by NAI. All payments will be made in United States dollars, free of any taxes then currently applicable, at the address designated above by NAI. Late payments shall bear interest at a rate determined by NAI from the due date until paid.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the payment terms to be determined by NAI introduces uncertainty and potential unfairness to the Co-Host, violating California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Removing a set timeframe for payments may enable predatory lending practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2305 allows parties to conclude a contract for sale even if the price is not settled. However, the code stipulates that the price must be reasonable if it is left to be fixed by one party. By removing the initial 30-day due date and interest rate limit, the contract contradicts this section, as it allows NAI to set unreasonable payment terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2305 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2305. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) Nothing is said as to price; or (b) The price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) The price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as canceled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Commercial Code § 2305, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original contract specified a 30-day payment term and a maximum interest rate. The modified contract replaces these with terms 'determined by NAI,' effectively allowing NAI to set unreasonable payment terms. Snippet 1 states that if 'the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree' or 'the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded,' the price should be a reasonable one. Removing the initial payment terms and interest rate limit allows NAI to potentially set unreasonable payment terms, as the changed contract no longer ensures a reasonable price or payment term. This constitutes a clear contradiction, as the modified clause removes consumer protection by failing to guarantee payment terms are reasonable and predictable. Therefore, there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. EQUITABLE RELIEF. Each party acknowledges that any breach of its obligations under this Agreement with respect to the proprietary rights or confidential information of the other party will cause the other party irreparable injury for which there are inadequate remedies at law, and therefore such other party will be entitled to equitable relief in addition to all other remedies provided by this Agreement or available at law.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. EQUITABLE RELIEF. Each party acknowledges that any breach of its obligations under this Agreement will cause the other party injury for which there are remedies at law, and therefore such other party may be entitled to legal relief in addition to other remedies provided by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'proprietary rights or confidential information', 'irreparable injury', and deleting equitable relief the contract attempts to limit the remedies available, this could violate California law regarding the availability of injunctive relief and specific performance in cases involving breach of contract, making the clause potentially unenforceable and introducing legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 outlines specific instances where injunctive relief (a form of equitable relief) may be granted, including situations where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. By removing the explicit right to equitable relief and focusing solely on legal remedies, the modified contract contradicts this section, potentially preventing a party from seeking necessary injunctive relief to protect their interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit mention of 'proprietary rights or confidential information' and 'irreparable injury', and replaces 'equitable relief' with 'legal relief'. This contradicts California Civil Code § 3422, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which allows for injunctive relief (a form of equitable relief) when 'pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief'. By omitting the possibility of equitable relief, the contract limits the remedies available to a party and could prevent them from seeking necessary injunctive relief, therefore a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: (e) This Agreement will be governed and interpreted according to the laws of the State of California, without reference to principles of conflicts of laws. Each party hereto expressly consents to the personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in Santa Clara County, California, and expressly waives any defense to any action based on inconvenient forum, choice of venue, lack of personal jurisdiction, sufficiency of service of process or the like.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) This Agreement will be governed and interpreted according to the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by NAI, without reference to principles of conflicts of laws. Each party consents to the jurisdiction chosen by NAI.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to be unilaterally determined by NAI creates an imbalance of power and could violate California's principles of fairness and reasonableness in contract law. It could also violate California Consumer Rights law because that makes it more difficult for the other party to resolve disputes fairly and according to the laws of the state where they are located and where the initial agreement was established. This introduces legal risk by potentially making the agreement unenforceable in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Rights Law - general principle of fairness and reasonableness
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly contradicting a specific section, this change runs afoul of California's general consumer protection principles, particularly the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Cal. Civ. Code § 1770 lists unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices. While this is not a direct consumer contract, the principle extends to business relationships where undue influence or unfairness is present. Forcing one party to accept a jurisdiction solely chosen by the other could be seen as an unfair or deceptive practice.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California's consumer protection principles, specifically regarding fairness and good faith. By allowing NAI to unilaterally choose the governing law and jurisdiction, the changed text undermines the consumer's (or other party's) ability to resolve disputes fairly under California law. The Law Explanation describes this as potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on deceptive practices in consumer transactions (Cal. Civ. Code § 1770(a)), the principle extends to business relationships where undue influence or unfairness exists. The original contract ensured a neutral forum (Santa Clara County, California) and adherence to California law, protecting both parties. The changed text removes this protection, granting NAI excessive control and creating an imbalance of power, which contradicts the principle of fairness.
